nav.rulesStandard: Sign language standard and sign variants
rules.standard.title: Sign language standard and sign variants
rules.standard.lead: About the unified sign standard and regional / usage variants.
rules.standard.p1: Mongolian Sign Language (MSL) is an independent language with its own rules of formation and orientation. Every sign on this platform follows a unified standard reviewed and approved by teachers.
rules.standard.p2: A single word is sometimes signed slightly differently across regions and schools. These are called “variants” and are shown next to the primary sign so learners can recognise all of them.
rules.standard.p3: The standard is chosen in consultation with teachers at School #29 and representatives of the deaf community, taking the most widely used form as primary. This principle is followed for every new word.
rules.structure.title: Sign structure
rules.structure.lead: How a sign is built — handshape, location, movement, orientation.
rules.structure.p1: Every sign is made of a few core parameters: handshape (finger position), the location on the body where it is made, the movement of the hand, and palm orientation.
rules.structure.p2: Changing any one of these parameters can change the whole meaning. That is why it is important to perform the shape, location and movement clearly and correctly.
rules.structure.p3: The dictionary filters let you search signs by parameters such as location and movement, which helps when comparing and learning similar signs.
rules.mouthing.title: Mouthing and lip shapes
rules.mouthing.lead: How mouthing and lip shapes help clarify the meaning of a sign.
rules.mouthing.p1: Many signs are accompanied by a specific mouth movement or lip shape alongside the hand movement. This is not spoken sound but a visual cue that distinguishes meaning.
rules.mouthing.p2: Mouthing helps tell apart two signs that look similar in shape. So when learning a sign, pay attention to the mouth shape together with the hand movement.
rules.mouthing.p3: Videos on this platform are recorded to show mouthing clearly, and a text description always accompanies them. Audio is never required — all information is visual.
